dialedInUi.toolGuardrail: Technical suggestion only — final machine adjustment is the artist’s responsibility.
dialedInUi.heroSubtitle: Learn to set up your tattoo machine, and the logic behind it.
dialedInUi.heroLede: Master your machine, no matter the brand. Get pro-level voltage, needle, and setup guidance for your style—designed for artists who value precision over hype.
dialedInUi.step1Body: Select your style. This establishes the physics required for the skin you are working on.
dialedInUi.step2Body: Define the hit (Lining vs Shading) to activate our safety guard against skin trauma.
dialedInUi.step3Body: Pick your machine—whether it's a Tier 2 workhorse or a Tier 1 benchmark. The engine adjusts to your specific hardware limits.
dialedInUi.step4Body: Use the Hand Speed slider to match your movement to the motor frequency. Watch the CPS gauge to ensure you aren't outrunning your needle.
dialedInUi.strokeBaselineHint: Recommended stroke baseline for {style}: {mm} mm — when you pick a machine, stroke snaps to the closest available option.
dialedInUi.handSpeedAriaLabel: Hand speed relative to machine cycle rate
dialedInUi.handSpeedNote: Note: Hand speed is a relative variable. This scale is designed to demonstrate the conceptual relationship between movement and cycle frequency, not as a literal measurement.
dialedInUi.gaugePlaceholder: Select style, technique, and machine to load recommended readouts and gauges.
dialedInUi.hzCaptionAcus: For ACUS, treat Hz as the main operating readout; voltage is a bench supply reference into that band.
dialedInUi.hzCaptionOther: Heuristic band tied to voltage—similar to how many bench supplies frame the drive readout.
dialedInUi.cpsCaption: CPS = round((final volts × 1000) ÷ 60)—a scalar teaching view of how hard the pack is being driven after hand-speed offset and clamp.
dialedInUi.lessonBody: DialedIn has adapted your voltage for a {currentStroke} mm stroke. For {style}, the industry standard is {idealStroke} mm to ensure optimal saturation without skin trauma.
dialedInUi.lessonLink: Learn more about the 3.5mm pivot and stroke physics.
dialedInUi.sltContextBody: Brand bridge equivalents (SLT, #10, TX) appear in glossary hovers on matching cartridge copy — main UI stays standard-first.
dialedInUi.voltDetailAdapted: Operating band {vmin} – {vmax} V (3.5 mm pivot offset {mod} V).
dialedInUi.voltDetailBaseline: Machine baseline {baseline} V → soft-guard / stroke adjusted {adj} V
dialedInUi.proTipRealism: Using a 4.2mm stroke for realism requires a very light hand — let needle work do the heavy lifting.
dialedInUi.proTipLongStroke: Long stroke (4.0mm+) with Soft Shading: baseline voltage is reduced by 1.5V to limit skin trauma.
dialedInUi.proTipHang: Higher hanging with soft techniques: float the needle and watch ink saturation — avoid over-driving voltage.
dialedInUi.recommendedPathHint: Recommended path is highlighted when both style and machine are set.
dialedInUi.needleHangDisclaimer: While we recommend brand-standard needle hang, setup remains a personal choice dictated by technical skill and artistic intent.

common.operatorNote: DialedIn.ink is operated by Albor Digital LLC. These pages are the public summary aligned with Albor Digital legal documentation.

disclaimer.title: Technical Disclaimer
disclaimer.lead: DialedIn is an educational and decision-support tool intended solely for informational purposes. Suggested parameters (voltage, hertz, stroke, and needle configuration) reflect general industry consensus and manufacturer specifications; they do not replace professional judgment or training under a qualified mentor.
disclaimer.acknowledge: By using this tool, you acknowledge that:
disclaimer.riskBody: Tattooing involves invasive procedures. Incorrect machine use or inappropriate settings can cause severe skin trauma or infections.
disclaimer.equipBody: Machines and cartridges differ by brand and maintenance condition; DialedIn does not guarantee exact outcomes.
disclaimer.liabilityBody: Albor Digital LLC is not liable for injuries, legal claims, or unsatisfactory aesthetic results. Final machine adjustment remains solely the artist’s responsibility.

privacy.title: Privacy & Data Transparency
privacy.lead: At DialedIn, we value your craft and your privacy.
privacy.dataBody: We do not request sensitive personal information. We only store configuration preferences anonymously to improve our logic engine.
privacy.aiBody: This tool uses language models to process technical taxonomies. We do not process personal data through these models.
privacy.cookiesBody: We use essential cookies to remember your language and equipment preferences for the session.

terms.title: Guild Rules (Terms of Service)
terms.useBody: This tool is for individual use by apprentices and tattoo professionals. Bulk extraction (scraping) of our technical logic is prohibited.
terms.ipBody: The relational logic and interface design are the exclusive property of Albor Digital LLC.
terms.lawBody: Any legal dispute shall be governed by the laws of the State of Wyoming, United States.

responsibleAi.title: Responsible AI Policy
responsibleAi.lead: DialedIn.ink is operated by Albor Digital LLC. This policy describes how we use automation and language models transparently.
responsibleAi.purposeBody: DialedIn combines deterministic rules and documented heuristics with optional language-model assistance for technical taxonomy labeling. It is not a medical device and must not replace professional judgment or manufacturer documentation.
responsibleAi.humanBody: Artists must validate every recommendation against manufacturer specs, skin type, session goals, and local regulations. The tool surfaces warnings where modeled checks flag potential mismatch.
responsibleAi.dataBody: We do not send identifiable personal data through language models for profiling. Taxonomy and preference handling follow this Privacy summary and essential cookie practices.
responsibleAi.accuracyBody: We version logic and publish a visible last-update stamp on the site. Material model changes will be reflected in release notes or methodology pages where practical.

blogNav.articleFooter: Published by DialedIn.ink team. Technical data last updated April 2026.

blogIndex.pageSubhead: Technical insights and machine physics from the DialedIn team.

membraneTax.summary: Learn why stiffer cartridge membranes can absorb effective drive and how to compensate voltage without overdriving skin, especially on tier-2 machines.

sciencePage.lede: Methodology for the DialedIn.ink authority layer: how stroke, voltage, taper geometry, and hand velocity are modeled together—not as isolated “settings,” but as coupled physical constraints.

universalMachinePhysics.p1: The DialedIn setup model operates on relational physics. Unlike linear calculators, it accounts for the Hammer Effect—the exponential increase in kinetic impact when long-stroke machines are used at high voltages. By integrating the ACUS standard, artists can transition from traditional voltage to frequency-based (Hz) tattooing while physical reciprocation (CPS) stays consistent across hardware tiers.
universalMachinePhysics.p2: Cartridge build quality also changes required drive. Lower-cost cartridges often use stiffer membranes, which increase mechanical resistance at the same nominal settings. The model compensates by keeping voltage, taper class, and hand-speed/CPS coupling in one system, so artists can make controlled voltage adjustments instead of over-driving the machine.

strokeCycle.p1: Rotary tattoo machines convert electrical cycles into needle displacement. For a given stroke length (mm), the needle travels a fixed arc per revolution. When hand velocity exceeds the rate at which those cycles deposit pigment, the skin sees under-filled segments—often misread as a "machine problem" when it is a velocity mismatch. The DialedIn.ink engine models this by pairing a seven-tier hand-speed scale with recommended voltage bands: faster hands generally require higher effective cycle energy, bounded by tissue safety envelopes.
strokeCycle.p2: Long-stroke machines (typically 4.0 mm+) store more kinetic energy in each impact. During soft shading or whip-style passes, that extra momentum raises the risk of hammer effect trauma unless voltage is deliberately pulled down—our Hammer Effect Guard applies a −1.5 V offset when stroke ≥ 4.0 mm and the technique is delicate shading-class.

acusParadigm.h2: The ACUS Paradigm: Frequency vs. Potential
acusParadigm.p1: With the arrival of the ACUS M1 and M2, the industry is moving from 'Voltage-thinking' to 'Frequency-thinking.' While traditional power supplies measure the electrical potential (Volts), the ACUS system prioritizes the Cycles Per Second (Hertz).
acusParadigm.p2: DialedIn.ink is engineered to bridge this gap. Our model translates standard motor constants into physical reciprocation rates. This ensures that whether you are using a standard rotary or an ACUS, your Hand Speed Sync remains mathematically consistent. By calculating physical CPS, artists can internalize the 'Speed of the Hit' independent of power-supply branding.

needleTaper.p1: Taper defines how quickly the needle group narrows from body to tip. SLT (super long taper) reduces frontal skin displacement for the same gauge, which matters for micro-detail and tight line work where standard long tapers can wedge tissue sideways. The Geometry Desync check flags Fine Line workflows where the resolved taper recommendation does not center SLT, prompting artists to reconsider cartridge geometry before chasing voltage or speed changes.
needleTaper.p2: TX (textured) surfaces increase effective pigment film on the needle—useful for large fields or low-viscosity inks—while changing how ink releases during the same stroke length. Treat texture as a geometry modifier, not a substitute for correct taper class.

gaugeTension.p1: Needle gauge (for example #10 at ~0.30 mm) sets the contact patch width. Smaller gauges reduce per-pass trauma but demand cleaner cycle timing: the skin's elastic return (tension + thickness) fights the needle on entry and exit. When tension is high—think convex surfaces or dehydrated skin—prefer slightly longer tapers and conservative voltage, then adjust Hanging (needle extension beyond the tip) before chasing larger groupings. More hang increases throw for packing but amplifies lateral spread if taper geometry is mismatched. DialedIn.ink keeps hang, taper, and voltage in one relational surface so one input change recomputes the full recommendation set.
gaugeTension.p2: For bold linework where tips are not pinched into a tight round, Open Liner geometry (parallel grouping) can reduce binding at the cost of finer edge sharpness—pair with appropriate stroke length and voltage, not maximum aggression.

howItWorksPage.heroLede: Precision is not an accident. DialedIn translates the physics of your machine into a calculated roadmap for your art.

step1.title: Style
step1.body: You name the craft—fine line, American Traditional, soft grey-wash. That choice sets the baseline needle frequency (CPS) and voltage window, not a generic default.

step2.title: Machine & stroke
step2.body: Stroke length and cartridge tier change how power reaches the skin. The engine syncs those variables to your hand so rotary machine stroke physics stay accountable.

step3.title: Result
step3.body: You get a voltage-to-CPS readout inside a safe operating band—the tattoo machine sweet spot—prioritizing skin health and motor longevity over reckless power.

styleFirst.label: Style-first logic
styleFirst.body: Every artistic intent has a technical baseline. We do not flatten everything into one chart. DialedIn uses a curated taxonomy where styles such as Fine Line or American Traditional anchor needle frequency (CPS) and voltage windows—so tattoo machine setup logic follows the standard of that discipline.

adaptiveStroke.label: Adaptive stroke sync
adaptiveStroke.beforePivot: Your machine's stroke is its physical punch. We treat about
adaptiveStroke.afterPivot: as the pivot between a shorter throw and a long-stroke hammer (4.0 mm and above). For soft work on a long throw, the engine lowers voltage to guard against skin trauma; on a short throw (around 2.5 mm), it can raise power so you still achieve reliable penetration.

hardwareTier.label: Hardware tiering & resistance
hardwareTier.body: Not all gear is equal. Budget cartridges often run stiffer membranes that steal power before the needle moves—the membrane tax. Hardware tiering adds a measured nudge so your motor is less likely to stall when the membrane fights back.

safety.label: Safety guardrails
safety.beforeLink: Technical accountability means every suggestion stays inside a safe operating range. Skin trauma prevention and respect for the motor come before chasing maximum volts. For the underlying research framing, see

p1.title: Style-first baseline
p1.body: Fine line, American Traditional, and soft shading do not share the same CPS and impact profile. The anchor is always the craft you selected—including how we think about fine line tattoo voltage versus bolder packing.

p2.title: Stroke & hand
p2.body: Stroke is throw length in millimeters. The same dial on a 4.2 mm machine hits harder than on a 3.0 mm rig—so voltage is never read in isolation from rotary machine stroke physics.

p3.title: Calibrated result
p3.body: Recommended voltage shifts so effective force on the skin stays coherent when you change machine, stroke, or tier—aligned with a tattoo needle grouping guide that matches your technique.

sec01.title: Style-first baseline
sec01.body: We don't believe in one size fits all. DialedIn starts by looking at your artistic goal. Whether you are pulling bold lines or layering soft grey-wash, the engine sets a baseline frequency (CPS) that matches the industry standard for that specific craft.

sec02.title: The stroke modifier (adaptive mode)
sec02.body: Your machine's stroke is its physical punch. A long-stroke machine (4.0 mm and up) moves with more momentum. To prevent skin trauma, DialedIn automatically dials back your voltage when you use a long stroke for soft techniques. Conversely, if your stroke is short, we nudge the power up to ensure you get the penetration you need.

sec03.title: Constant calculation
sec03.body: As you change your machine or your style, the engine recalculates in real time. It's like having a technical mentor at your station, ensuring your gear is always synced to your hand.
sec03.noteMid: : frequency and volts are partners, not duplicates. DialedIn keeps them in one context—see also
sec03.noteBlogLink: hertz and volts on the machine

sec04.title: The membrane tax, in plain terms
sec04.beforeLink: Stiffer membranes add resistance before meaningful needle motion. That is why some budget cartridges feel like they tax your motor. DialedIn accounts for tier differences with a small compensating nudge so the pull stays honest. Deeper read:

sec05.title: What disciplined setup buys you
sec05.li1: Controlled trauma: Matching CPS to hand speed and stroke reduces scatter from fighting your own motor rhythm.
sec05.li2: Predictable saturation: Fewer hesitant passes from guessing voltage in a vacuum.
sec05.li3: Standards, not slogans: Entry-level rotaries and flagship machines answer to the same physics—the tattoo machine sweet spot is a method, not a brand story.

sec06.title: Scientific methodology: the constant of effective force
sec06.p1: Our logic is built on the physical relationship between stroke length (S) and needle frequency (F). As S increases, the impact force on the skin increases exponentially. To maintain a constant safe impact zone, frequency (voltage) must be inversely adjusted.
sec06.beforeStrokeLink: DialedIn uses a linear decay formula around the
sec06.betweenLinks: to keep your machine in the mechanical sweet spot, regardless of hardware tiering. For the full stroke narrative, see

message.HAMMER_EFFECT: Kinetic Impact Hazard: A 4.0mm+ stroke delivers high needle momentum. Reducing voltage by 1.5V is required to prevent the 'Hammer Effect' (sub-dermal scarring) during delicate shading.
message.GEOMETRY_DESYNC: Geometry Mismatch: Standard tapers create excessive skin displacement for micro-details. Super Long Taper (SLT) is recommended to prevent ink spread (blowouts).
message.VELOCITY_SYNC: Frequency/Velocity Desync: Your hand speed exceeds the machine's cycle rate. This may cause 'dashed' lines or skin snagging. Increase voltage or decelerate hand movement.

blogRuleBreakingExit.toolPrompt: Check your specific machine setup for this technique:

lineage.liner: Grouping window biased toward single-file / round-liner work.
lineage.magnum: Grouping window biased toward shader / magnum-class coverage.
lineage.balanced: Grouping window spans multiple valid cartridge families for this style.

needleScale.scaleLogicLinkAria: Open technical explanation of scale-dependent needle count
needleScale.modalIntro: How coverage, density, and anatomy interact with the numbers on the box.
needleScale.modalBody: Technical logic: Our count recommendation is scale-agnostic. While technique dictates the grouping type (e.g., magnum for shading), the specific count should scale with the size of the artwork. Use larger counts for broad surfaces to ensure tonal consistency and efficiency; use smaller counts for restricted anatomical areas.
needleScale.fillNote: Note: Final count depends on the surface area you intend to cover and the speed of your fill.
needleScale.traumaInline: Wide bundles amplify mechanical load across many points—in thin or high‑tension areas, efficiency must track stretch, angle, and speed. Use [Scale logic] for the full rationale.
needleScale.traumaBody: Very high counts (e.g. 25–27+ magnum-class bundles) move a lot of ink per pass; on thin, high-tension, or highly curved skin the same mechanical efficiency can over-stress tissue if stretch and angle drift. Naming the tradeoff matters.
